translations.literal: Later Sheng was denounced by a divorced wife, sat in guilt and was imprisoned, obtained release, and returned to his village.
translations.idiomatic: Later his ex-wife denounced him; he was jailed, freed, and went home.

sentences.zh: 永平中，為聊城令，坐事免，卒於家。

translations.literal: In the Yongping era he was magistrate of Liaocheng, sat in guilt on an affair and was dismissed, and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yongping he was prefect of Liaocheng, lost office over a case, and died at home.

sentences.zh: 陳元字長孫，蒼梧廣信人也。

translations.literal: Chen Yuan, courtesy name Changsun, was a man of Guangxin in Cangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yuan, style Changsun, came from Guangxin in Cangwu.

sentences.zh: [一]父欽，習左氏春秋，事黎陽賈護，與劉歆同時而別自名家。

translations.literal: [One] His father Qin studied the Zuo Spring and Autumn, served Jia Hu of Liyang, and was contemporary with Liu Xin yet separately became a master in his own name.
translations.idiomatic: His father Chen Qin studied Zuo under Jia Hu of Liyang, Liu Xin's contemporary, yet founded his own school.

sentences.zh: [二]王莽從欽受左氏學，以欽為猒難將軍。

translations.literal: [Two] Wang Mang received Zuo learning from Qin and appointed Qin general who subdues difficulty.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang studied Zuo with Chen Qin and made him general who subdues difficulty.

sentences.zh: [三]元少傳父業，為之訓詁，銳精覃思，至不與鄉里通。

translations.literal: [Three] Yuan in youth transmitted his father's enterprise, glossed it for him, sharpened his thought exhaustively, to the point of not associating with his village.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yuan took his father's glosses, refined them obsessively, and shunned local society.

sentences.zh: 以父任為郎。

translations.literal: By his father's privilege he became gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: He entered the court as a gentleman by his father's rank.

sentences.zh: 注[一]廣信故城在今梧州蒼梧縣。

translations.literal: Note one: the old city of Guangxin is in present-day Cangwu county of Wuzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Guangxin stood in modern Cangwu, Wuzhou.

sentences.zh: 注[二]元父欽，字子佚。

translations.literal: Note two: Yuan's father Qin, courtesy name Ziyi.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Chen Qin's style was Ziyi.

sentences.zh: 以左氏授王莽，自名陳氏春秋，故曰別也。

translations.literal: He transmitted the Zuo to Wang Mang and himself named it Chen's Spring and Autumn, hence 'separate.'
translations.idiomatic: He taught Zuo to Wang Mang but called his version Chen's Spring and Autumn—hence a distinct line.

sentences.zh: 賈護字季君。

translations.literal: Jia Hu, courtesy name Jijun.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Hu, style Jijun.

sentences.zh: 並見前書也。

translations.literal: Both appear in the former Han book.
translations.idiomatic: Both are recorded in the Hanshu.

sentences.zh: 注[三]猒，一葉反。

translations.literal: Note three: yan, one reading ye entering tone.
translations.idiomatic: Note: read the general's title-name with ye tone.

sentences.zh: 建武初，元與桓譚、杜林、鄭興俱為學者所宗。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Jianwu, Yuan together with Huan Tan, Du Lin, and Zheng Xing were all taken as authorities by scholars.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Jianwu, Chen Yuan, Huan Tan, Du Lin, and Zheng Xing were the scholars the world looked to.

sentences.zh: 時議欲立左氏傳博士，范升奏以為左氏淺末，不宜立。

translations.literal: At that time deliberation wished to establish an erudite for the Zuo commentary; Fan Sheng memorialized that the Zuo was shallow and trivial and ought not to be established.
translations.idiomatic: When the court debated a Zuo chair, Fan Sheng called the tradition shallow and unworthy.

sentences.zh: 元聞之，乃詣闕上疏曰：

translations.literal: When Yuan heard this he therefore went to the palace gates and submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yuan went to the palace and wrote:

sentences.zh: 陛下撥亂反正，文武並用，[一]深愍經蓺謬雜，真偽錯亂，每臨朝日，輒延腢臣講論聖道。

translations.literal: Your Majesty has set straight the rebellion and restored rectitude, employing both civil and military means; [one] you deeply pity that the classics are confused and mixed, true and false tangled, and every court day you extend ministers to expound the sage way.
translations.idiomatic: You ended chaos and restored Han, pity how corrupt the canon had grown, and each audience you call ministers to expound the Way.

sentences.zh: 知丘明至賢，親受孔子，而公羊、谷梁傳聞於後世，故詔立左氏，博詢可否，示不專己，盡之腢下也。

translations.literal: Knowing that Qiu Ming was supremely worthy and personally received Confucius, while Gongyang and Guliang were hearsay from later ages, therefore you issued an edict to establish the Zuo, broadly asking whether it may be done, showing that you do not trust yourself alone but exhaust the matter to those below.
translations.idiomatic: You know Zuo Qiu Ming studied with Confucius while Gongyang and Guliang are later hearsay—so you asked broadly about a Zuo chair, showing you defer to the realm, not private whim.

sentences.zh: 今論者沉溺所習，翫守舊聞，固執虛言傳受之辭，以非親見實事之道。

translations.literal: Now debaters sink in what they are used to, play with guarding old hearsay, stubbornly hold empty phrases of transmitted reception, and thereby reject the way of personally witnessed fact.
translations.idiomatic: Critics cling to habit, toy with old glosses, and reject eyewitness tradition as hearsay.

sentences.zh: 左氏孤學少與，[二]遂為異家之所覆冒。

translations.literal: The Zuo is a solitary learning with few allies; [two] thereupon it is overlaid by other houses.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo stands almost alone—rival schools have smothered it.

sentences.zh: 夫至音不合觿聽，故伯牙絕弦；

translations.literal: The utmost music does not accord with vulgar hearing—therefore Boya broke his strings;
translations.idiomatic: Perfect music bores the crowd—Boya smashed his lute when none could hear;

sentences.zh: [三]至寶不同觿好，故卞和泣血。

translations.literal: [three] the utmost treasure does not accord with vulgar taste—therefore Bian He wept blood.
translations.idiomatic: a perfect jade offends common taste—Bian He wept blood at the wall;

sentences.zh: [四]仲尼聖德，而不容於世，[五]況於竹帛余文，其為雷同者所排，固其宜也。

translations.literal: [four] Confucius had sage virtue yet was not tolerated by the world; [five] how much more writings on bamboo and silk—what the mediocre herd rejects is naturally to be expected.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius himself was hounded—how much more will pedants reject his disciple's text on silk?

sentences.zh: 非陛下至明，孰能察之！

translations.literal: Were it not for Your Majesty's utmost clarity, who could discern this!
translations.idiomatic: Only a ruler as clear-sighted as you could see the truth.

sentences.zh: 注[一]撥，理也。

translations.literal: Note one: bo means to set in order.
translations.idiomatic: Note: bo luan fan zheng from Gongyang.

sentences.zh: 語見公羊傳。

translations.literal: The language appears in the Gongyang commentary.
translations.idiomatic: The phrase comes from the Gongyang.

sentences.zh: 注[二]與猶黨也。

translations.literal: Note two: yu means faction or party.
translations.idiomatic: Note: yu here means allies or party.

sentences.zh: 注[三]伯牙善鼓琴，鐘子期善聽，相與為友。

translations.literal: Note three: Boya was skilled at playing the zither; Zhong Ziqi was skilled at listening; they became friends.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Boya and Zhong Ziqi were zither and ear.

sentences.zh: 子期死，伯牙破琴絕弦，不復鼓琴，以時人莫之能聽也。

translations.literal: When Ziqi died, Boya broke his zither and cut his strings and never again played the zither, because none of his time could hear him.
translations.idiomatic: When Ziqi died, Boya broke his strings—no one else could listen.

sentences.zh: 見呂覽。

translations.literal: See the Lü compendium.
translations.idiomatic: See Lüshi chunqiu.

sentences.zh: 注[四]卞和得寶玉，獻楚武王，王示玉人，曰「石也」，刖其右足。

translations.literal: Note four: Bian He obtained a precious jade and presented it to King Wu of Chu; the king showed it to the jade craftsman who said 'It is stone' and cut off his right foot.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Bian He's first gift to Chu was called stone; they cut his right foot.

sentences.zh: 武王歿後，復獻之文王，復曰「石也」，刖其左足。

translations.literal: After King Wu died he again presented it to King Wen, who again said 'It is stone' and cut off his left foot.
translations.idiomatic: King Wen's court also called it stone and took his left foot.

sentences.zh: 至成王時，卞和抱其璞於郊，泣盡以血繼之，王乃使玉尹攻之，果得寶玉。

translations.literal: When it came to King Cheng's time Bian He embraced his uncut block in the suburbs and wept until blood followed his tears; the king then ordered a jade master to work it and indeed obtained the precious jade.
translations.idiomatic: At King Cheng's reign he wept blood at the jade block until the king had it cut—true jade emerged.

sentences.zh: 事見韓子也。

translations.literal: The affair appears in Han Feizi.
translations.idiomatic: See Han Feizi.

sentences.zh: 注[五]仲尼去魯，斥齊，逐乎宋、□，困於陳、蔡之閒。

translations.literal: Note five: Confucius left Lu, was driven from Qi, pressed in Song and Wei, and besieged between Chen and Cai.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Confucius's wanderings through Qi, Song, Wei, Chen, and Cai.

sentences.zh: 見史記。

translations.literal: See the Records of the Historian.
translations.idiomatic: See Shiji.

sentences.zh: 臣元竊見博士范升等所議奏左氏春秋不可立，及太史公違戾凡四十五事。

translations.literal: Your servant Yuan has privately seen that Erudite Fan Sheng and others in their deliberated memorial said the Zuo Spring and Autumn ought not to be established, and that the Grand Historian contradicted orthodoxy in forty-five matters altogether.
translations.idiomatic: I have read Fan Sheng's forty-five counts against Zuo and Sima Qian.

sentences.zh: 案升為所言，前後相違，皆斷□小文，媟黷微辭，以年數小差，掇為巨謬，[一]遺脫纖微，指為大尤，抉瑕擿釁，[二]掩其弘美，所謂「小辯破言，小言破道」者也。

translations.literal: Examining what Sheng stated, front and back contradict; all break off small graphs, play with slight wording, take small errors in year counts and pick them up as huge mistakes, [one] omit fine points and call them great faults, pry out flaws and expose cracks, [two] cover their great beauty—this is what is called 'petty disputation breaks speech, petty speech breaks the way.'
translations.idiomatic: Fan Sheng's case is self-contradictory: he quibbles over digits and words, blows typos into heresy, hides Zuo's greatness—petty debate that breaks the Way.

sentences.zh: [三]升等又曰：「先帝不以左氏為經，故不置博士，後主所宜因襲。

translations.literal: [Three] Sheng and others also say: 'Former emperors did not treat Zuo as a classic, therefore did not establish an erudite; later rulers ought to follow that inheritance.'
translations.idiomatic: Fan Sheng says earlier Han refused Zuo a chair, so you must too.

sentences.zh: 」臣愚以為若先帝所行而後主必行者，則盤庚不當遷於殷，周公不當營洛邑，[四] 陛下不當都山東也。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks that if whatever former emperors did later rulers must do, then Pan Geng ought not to have moved to Yin, the Duke of Zhou ought not to have built Luoyang, [four] and Your Majesty ought not to have made your capital east of the mountains.
translations.idiomatic: If sons may never change fathers' policy, Pan Geng would never have moved the capital, the Duke of Zhou would never have built Luoyang, and you would never have left the eastern plain.

sentences.zh: 往者，孝武皇帝好公羊，□太子好谷梁，有詔詔太子受公羊，不得受谷梁。

translations.literal: Formerly Emperor Xiaowu loved Gongyang, and Crown Prince Ju loved Guliang; there was an edict ordering the heir to receive Gongyang and not to receive Guliang.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu favored Gongyang; Crown Prince Ju favored Guliang—an edict forced the heir to study Gongyang, not Guliang.

sentences.zh: 孝宣皇帝在人閒時，聞□太子好谷梁，於是獨學之。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xiaoxuan was among the people he heard that Crown Prince Ju loved Guliang and therefore studied it alone.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi, still a commoner, heard that Crown Prince Ju had loved Guliang and studied that tradition himself.

sentences.zh: 及即位，為石渠論而谷梁氏興，[五]至今與公羊並存。

translations.literal: When he ascended the throne he held the Shiqu discussions and the Guliang lineage rose; [five] to this day it exists together with Gongyang.
translations.idiomatic: On the throne he held the Shiqu conference and raised Guliang—it still stands beside Gongyang.

sentences.zh: 此先帝后帝各有所立，不必其相因也。

translations.literal: This is former and later emperors each establishing what they chose; they need not follow one another.
translations.idiomatic: Each emperor chose his own orthodoxy; one need not ape the last.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰，純，儉，吾從觿；

translations.literal: Confucius said: as to pure silk, frugality—I follow the multitude;
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said he would follow the crowd on cheaper silk caps;

sentences.zh: 至於拜下，則違之。

translations.literal: as for bowing below, then I oppose it.
translations.idiomatic: on bowing on the stair below, he stood firm.

sentences.zh: [六]夫明者獨見，不惑於朱紫，聽者獨聞，不謬於清濁，故離朱不為巧眩移目，[七]師曠不為新聲易耳。

translations.literal: [Six] The clear-sighted see alone and are not deluded by red and purple; the keen-eared hear alone and are not mistaken in clear and muddy; therefore Li Zhu was not moved in the eye by clever dazzlement, [seven] and Music-master Kuang did not change his ear for new sounds.
translations.idiomatic: The clear eye sorts crimson from purple; the true ear tells pure from muddy—Li Zhu was not fooled by tricks of light, nor Kuang by novelty.

sentences.zh: [八]方今干戈少弭，戎事略戰，留思聖蓺，眷顧儒雅，采孔子拜下之義，卒淵聖獨見之旨，分明白黑，建立左氏，解釋先聖之積結，洮汰學者之累惑，[九] 使基業垂於萬世，後進無復狐疑，則天下幸甚。

translations.literal: [Eight] Now weapons are slightly laid aside, military affairs roughly stilled; attend to the sage classics, look back on Confucian elegance, take Confucius's meaning of bowing below, fulfill the profound sage's intent of seeing alone, divide white from black, establish the Zuo, unravel the former sages' knotted problems, wash away the scholars' accumulated doubts, [nine] let the foundation hang for ten thousand generations so that later students need no longer hesitate—then the realm will be greatly fortunate.
translations.idiomatic: Weapons rest; turn to the canon, follow Confucius's humility, choose truth from falsehood, establish Zuo, clear the sages' tangles, rinse away doubt—then the dynasty's learning will rest on bedrock.

sentences.zh: 注[一]媟，狎也；

translations.literal: Note one: xie means familiar trifling;
translations.idiomatic: Note: xie is cheap familiarity;

sentences.zh: 黷，垢濁也。

translations.literal: du means foul and turbid.
translations.idiomatic: du means smearing filth.

sentences.zh: 掇，拾也，音丁括反。

translations.literal: duo means to pick up; the reading is duo, entering with ding initial.
translations.idiomatic: Duo means to pluck out; read it duo.

sentences.zh: 注[二]抉音於決反。

translations.literal: Note two: jue is read yu jue departing.
translations.idiomatic: Note: jue read in the yu jue cut.

sentences.zh: 注[三]大戴記小辯篇孔子曰：「小辯破言，小言破義，小義破道。」

translations.literal: Note three: the Da Dai record, chapter Petty disputation, Confucius says: 'Petty disputation breaks speech, petty speech breaks righteousness, petty righteousness breaks the way.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: Da Dai liji on petty debate.

sentences.zh: 注[四]盤庚都耿，自耿遷於殷。

translations.literal: Note four: Pan Geng had his capital at Geng and moved from Geng to Yin.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Pan Geng moved from Geng to Yin.

sentences.zh: 文王都酆，武王都鎬，周公輔成王營洛邑。

translations.literal: King Wen had his capital at Feng, King Wu at Hao, and the Duke of Zhou assisted King Cheng in building Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Wen ruled from Feng, Wu from Hao; the Duke of Zhou built Luoyang for Cheng.

sentences.zh: 注[五]石渠閣以藏秘書，在未央殿北。

translations.literal: Note five: the Shiqu gallery stored secret books, north of the Weiyang hall.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Shiqu library north of Weiyang.

sentences.zh: 宣帝甘露三年，詔諸儒韋玄成、梁丘賀等講論五經於石渠也。

translations.literal: In the third year of the Ganlu era of Emperor Xuan an edict ordered the various scholars Wei Xuancheng, Liangqiu He, and others to expound the five classics at Shiqu.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi's Ganlu 3 conference at Shiqu debated the five classics.

sentences.zh: 注[六]論語孔子曰：「麻冕，禮也。

translations.literal: Note six: Confucius in the Analects said: 'The hemp cap is ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Analects on the hemp cap;

sentences.zh: 今也純，儉，吾從觿。

translations.literal: Now it is pure silk, economical—I follow the multitude.
translations.idiomatic: today's silk cap is cheaper—Confucius followed the crowd;

sentences.zh: 拜下，禮也。

translations.literal: Bowing below is ritual.
translations.idiomatic: bowing from below was ritual;

sentences.zh: 今拜乎上，泰也。

translations.literal: Now they bow above—this is arrogance.
translations.idiomatic: bowing only above is arrogance;

sentences.zh: 雖違觿，吾從下。

translations.literal: Though it opposes the multitude, I follow the lower.'
translations.idiomatic: yet he said he would still bow low."

sentences.zh: 」何晏注云：「麻冕，緇布冠也，古績麻三十升以為之。

translations.literal: He Yan's commentary says: 'The hemp cap is the black cloth cap; in antiquity they wove hemp thirty sheng thick to make it.
translations.idiomatic: He Yan glosses the hemp cap and thirty-sheng weave;

sentences.zh: 純，絲也。

translations.literal: chun means silk.
translations.idiomatic: chun means silk;

sentences.zh: 絲易成，故從儉。

translations.literal: silk is easy to make, therefore he followed economy.
translations.idiomatic: silk is easier to weave, hence economy;

sentences.zh: 臣之與君行禮者，下拜然後升。

translations.literal: between minister and ruler in performing ritual one bows below then ascends.
translations.idiomatic: ritual had the minister bow below the steps, then ascend;

sentences.zh: 時臣驕泰，故於上拜。

translations.literal: at that time ministers were arrogant and therefore bowed above.
translations.idiomatic: later pride made them bow only above;

sentences.zh: 今從下，禮之恭也。」

translations.literal: now to follow the lower is the courtesy of ritual.'"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius chose the humble bow."

sentences.zh: 注[七]離朱，黃帝時明目者也，一號離婁。

translations.literal: Note seven: Li Zhu was a clear-sighted man in the time of the Yellow Emperor, also called Li Lou.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Li Zhu or Li Lou, legendary sharp sight.

sentences.zh: 慎子曰：「離朱之明，察毫末於百步之外。」

translations.literal: Master Shen said: 'Li Zhu's clarity could examine the tip of a hair a hundred paces away.'"
translations.idiomatic: Shenzi on Li Zhu's hundred-pace sight.

sentences.zh: 注[八]桓譚新論曰：「晉師曠善知音，□靈公將之晉，宿於濮水之上，夜聞新聲，召師涓告之曰：『為我聽寫之。

translations.literal: Note eight: Huan Tan's New discourse says: 'Jin's Music-master Kuang was skilled at knowing tones; Duke Ling of Wei was going to Jin and lodged above the Pu River; at night he heard a new sound and summoned Music-master Juan and told him: "For me listen and write it down.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Huan Tan's story of Duke Ling, Juan, and Kuang at Pu;

sentences.zh: 』曰：『臣得之矣。

translations.literal: ' He said: 'Your servant has got it.
translations.idiomatic: Juan transcribed the tune;

sentences.zh: 』遂之晉。

translations.literal: ' Thereupon they went to Jin.
translations.idiomatic: they traveled on to Jin;

sentences.zh: 晉平公饗之，酒酣，靈公曰：『有新聲，願奏之。

translations.literal: Duke Ping of Jin feasted them; when the wine was warm Duke Ling said: 'There is a new sound; I wish it performed.
translations.idiomatic: at Ping's feast Ling asked for the new piece;

sentences.zh: 』乃令師涓鼓琴。

translations.literal: ' He then ordered Juan to play the zither.
translations.idiomatic: Juan played;

sentences.zh: 未終，師曠止之曰：『此亡國之聲也。

translations.literal: before it ended Kuang stopped it and said: 'This is the music of a state that perished.'
translations.idiomatic: Kuang stopped him: "That is a conqueror's tune."'

sentences.zh: 』」注[九]洮汰猶洗濯也。

translations.literal: '" Note nine: tao tai means to wash and scour.
translations.idiomatic: Note: tao tai means to rinse clean.

sentences.zh: 臣元愚鄙，嘗傳師言。

translations.literal: Your servant Yuan is foolish and low, yet has transmitted a teacher's words.
translations.idiomatic: I am a dull scholar, yet I pass my master's teaching.

sentences.zh: 如得以褐衣召見，俯伏庭下，[一]誦孔氏之正道，理丘明之宿冤；

translations.literal: If I may be summoned in coarse garb and prostrate below the courtyard, [one] I will recite Confucius's correct way and right Zuo Qiu Ming's long-standing wrong;
translations.idiomatic: Let me appear in humble dress in the courtyard, chant Confucius's orthodoxy, and clear Zuo Qiu Ming's old slander;

sentences.zh: 若辭不合經，事不稽古，退就重誅，雖死之日，生之年也。

translations.literal: if my words do not match the classics and affairs do not examine antiquity, let me retire to heavy punishment—even on the day of death it will be my day of life.
translations.idiomatic: if I lie against the canon, execute me—I will count death as life.

sentences.zh: 注[一]褐，織毛為布，貧者之服也。

translations.literal: Note one: he is cloth woven from wool, the garment of the poor.
translations.idiomatic: Note: he is coarse wool, a poor man's robe.

sentences.zh: 書奏，下其議，范升復與元相辯難，凡十餘上。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted the matter was sent down for discussion; Fan Sheng again debated with Yuan, altogether more than ten exchanges.
translations.idiomatic: The throne circulated the memorial; Fan Sheng and Chen Yuan traded ten-plus replies.

sentences.zh: 帝卒立左氏學，太常選博士四人，元為第一。

translations.literal: The emperor in the end established Zuo learning; the grand master of ceremonies selected four erudites, Yuan ranking first.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu created the Zuo chair; the grand master of ceremonies picked four erudites with Chen Yuan first.

sentences.zh: 帝以元新忿爭，乃用其次司隸從事李封，於是諸儒以左氏之立，論議讙嘩，自公卿以下，數廷爭之。

translations.literal: Because Yuan was newly contentious in temper, he used the next in line, clerk of the metropolitan commandant Li Feng, whereupon the various scholars, because the Zuo had been established, debated in uproar; from dukes and ministers downward they repeatedly disputed in court.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing Chen Yuan's temper, the emperor chose Li Feng instead; the court erupted—everyone from ministers down wrangled in session.

sentences.zh: 會封病卒，左氏復廢。

translations.literal: When Feng died of illness, Zuo learning was again abolished.
translations.idiomatic: When Li Feng died, the Zuo chair was struck down again.

sentences.zh: 元以才高著名，辟司空李通府。

translations.literal: Yuan was famous for lofty talent and was summoned to minister of works Li Tong's bureau.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yuan's fame won him Li Tong's bureau.

sentences.zh: 時大司農江馮上言，宜令司隸校尉督察三公。

translations.literal: At that time the grand minister of agriculture Jiang Feng submitted a statement that it was fitting to order the metropolitan commandant to supervise the three excellencies.
translations.idiomatic: Grand minister of agriculture Jiang Feng wanted the metropolitan commandant to oversee the three dukes.

sentences.zh: 事下三府。

translations.literal: The matter was sent down to the three bureaus.
translations.idiomatic: The edict went to the three offices.

sentences.zh: 元上疏曰：「臣聞師臣者帝，賓臣者霸。

translations.literal: Yuan submitted a memorial saying: 'Your servant has heard that he who takes ministers as teachers is emperor, he who takes ministers as guests is hegemon.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yuan answered: "Treat ministers as teachers and you are a true emperor; treat them as guests and you are a hegemon;

sentences.zh: [一]故武王以太公為師，齊桓以夷吾為仲父。

translations.literal: [One] Therefore King Wu took the grand duke as teacher, Duke Huan of Qi took Yiwu as elder uncle.
translations.idiomatic: King Wu made Jiang Taigong his teacher; Duke Huan made Guan Zhong his elder statesman.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『百官總己聽於頤宰。

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'The hundred offices each manage their own affairs and listen to the chief minister.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said the hundred bureaus answer to the chief minister;

sentences.zh: 』[二]近則高帝優相國之禮，[三]太宗假宰輔之權。

translations.literal: '[Two] In near times the high ancestor showed favor to the minister of state's rites; [three] Emperor Wen lent authority to his chief minister.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu honored Xiao He; Wendi empowered his chancellor;

sentences.zh: [四]及亡新王莽，遭漢中衰，專操國柄，以偷天下，[五] 況己自喻，不信腢臣。

translations.literal: [Four] When fallen Xin Wang Mang met Han in decline, monopolized the state's handle, and stole the realm, [five] took himself alone as the model and did not trust his ministers.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang seized a declining Han, he monopolized power, stole the throne, trusted only his own mirror, and refused his ministers.

sentences.zh: 奪公輔之任，損宰相之威，以刺舉為明，徼訐為直。

translations.literal: He stripped the three excellencies of their task, diminished the chancellor's awesomeness, took denunciation as clarity, and took malicious informing as uprightness.
translations.idiomatic: He gutted the three dukes, shrank the chancellor's prestige, and mistook informers for truth-tellers.

sentences.zh: 至乃陪僕告其君長，子弟變其父兄，[六]罔密法峻，大臣無所措手足。

translations.literal: To the point that attendants denounced their lords, sons and younger brothers turned on fathers and elder brothers; [six] the net was dense and the laws harsh, and great ministers had nowhere to put hand or foot.
translations.idiomatic: Attendants informed on superiors and sons turned on fathers until the laws tightened and ministers could not move.

sentences.zh: 然不能禁董忠之謀，身為世戮。

translations.literal: Yet he could not prevent Dong Zhong's plot and himself was slaughtered for the age.
translations.idiomatic: He still could not stop Dong Zhong's conspiracy and died in infamy.

sentences.zh: [七]故人君患在自驕，不患驕臣；

translations.literal: [Seven] Therefore the trouble of the ruler lies in self-pride, not in proud ministers;
translations.idiomatic: A ruler's fault is pride in himself, not pride in good ministers;

sentences.zh: 失在自任，不在任人。

translations.literal: the failure lies in trusting himself alone, not in trusting others.
translations.idiomatic: failure is trusting only oneself, not delegating.

sentences.zh: 是以文王有日□之勞，周公執吐握之恭，[八]不聞其崇刺舉，務督察也。

translations.literal: Therefore King Wen had the fatigue of sun and moon, the Duke of Zhou held the reverence of spitting out food and gripping hair; [eight] one does not hear that they exalted denunciation or devoted themselves to supervision.
translations.idiomatic: King Wen wore himself with audiences; the Duke of Zhou rushed meals and hair—neither built power on spies.

sentences.zh: 方今四方尚擾，天下未一，百姓觀聽，鹹張耳目。

translations.literal: Now the four quarters are still disturbed, the realm is not yet one, the hundred surnames watch and listen—all open ear and eye.
translations.idiomatic: The realm is still at war; the people watch every move;

sentences.zh: 陛下宜修文武之聖典，襲祖宗之遺德，勞心下士，屈節待賢，誠不宜使有司察公輔之名。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought to cultivate the sage canons of Wen and Wu, inherit the remaining virtue of the ancestors, labor the mind to humble yourself before scholars, bend your demeanor to await the worthy—it is truly not fitting to let responsible officials inspect the reputation of the three excellencies.'
translations.idiomatic: cultivate Wen and Wu's model, humble yourself to worthies, and never let a petty inspector judge the three dukes."

sentences.zh: 」帝從之，宣下其議。

translations.literal: The emperor followed it and proclaimed the discussion downward.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted and published the decision.

sentences.zh: [九] 注[一]言以臣為師，以臣為賓也。

translations.literal: [Nine] Note one: this means taking ministers as teachers and as guests.
translations.idiomatic: Note: emperors as learners, hegemons as hosts.

sentences.zh: 注[二]論語文也。

translations.literal: Note two: text of the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Analects wording.

sentences.zh: 注[三]蕭何為相國，高帝賜□履上殿，入朝不趨。

translations.literal: Note three: Xiao He as minister of state, the high ancestor granted him shoes to ascend the hall and to enter court without hurrying.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gaozu's honors to Xiao He.

sentences.zh: 注[四]太宗，孝文也。

translations.literal: Note four: Taizong is Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Taizong means Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 申屠嘉為丞相，坐府召太中大夫鄧通，欲誅之。

translations.literal: Shen Tujia as chancellor sat in his bureau and summoned grand palace counsellor Deng Tong, wishing to execute him.
translations.idiomatic: Shen Tujia tried to execute Deng Tong from the chancellor's office;

sentences.zh: 孝文使持節召通，令人謝嘉，故曰：「假權」也。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen sent a credential to summon Tong and ordered men to apologize to Jia, therefore it is called 'lent authority.'
translations.idiomatic: Wendi sent credentials to save Deng—hence "lent authority" to the chancellor.

sentences.zh: 注[五]偷，竊也。

translations.literal: Note five: tou means to steal.
translations.idiomatic: Note: tou means to usurp.

sentences.zh: 注[六]王莽時開吏告其將，奴婢告其主。

translations.literal: Note six: under Wang Mang he opened the way for clerks to denounce their generals and slave girls to denounce their masters.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Mang encouraged clerks to inform on generals and slaves on masters.

sentences.zh: 注[七]董忠為王莽大司馬，共劉歆等謀誅莽，事發覺死也。

translations.literal: Note seven: Dong Zhong was grand marshal of Xin for Wang Mang and together with Liu Xin and others plotted to kill Mang; when the affair was discovered he died.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Dong Zhong conspired with Liu Xin against Wang Mang and died when exposed.

sentences.zh: 注[八]尚書曰：「文王自朝至於日中昃，不遑暇食。

translations.literal: Note eight: the Documents says: 'King Wen from dawn to midday and past midday had no leisure to eat.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Documents on King Wen's diligence.

sentences.zh: 」史記曰，伯禽封魯，周公戒之曰：「我文王之子，武王之弟，成王之叔父，亦不賤矣。

translations.literal: The Records say: when Boqin was enfeoffed in Lu, the Duke of Zhou admonished him: 'I am a son of King Wen, younger brother of King Wu, uncle of King Cheng—this is not lowly.
translations.idiomatic: Shiji: the Duke of Zhou warned Boqin of Lu:

sentences.zh: 我一沐三握發，一飯三吐哺，以待士，猶恐失天下之賢人，汝無以國驕人也。」

translations.literal: At one bath I thrice grip my dripping hair; at one meal I thrice spit out my mouthful, to await scholars, and still fear losing the worthy of the realm—do not be arrogant because of the state.'"
translations.idiomatic: I break off bath and meal to meet talent—do not scorn others with your fief."

sentences.zh: 注[九]司察猶督察也。

translations.literal: Note nine: si cha means to supervise and inspect.
translations.idiomatic: Note: si cha means oversight.

sentences.zh: 李通罷，元後復辟司徒歐陽歙府，數陳當世便事、郊廟之禮，帝不能用。

translations.literal: When Li Tong stepped down, Yuan afterward was again summoned to minister over the masses Ouyang He's bureau; he repeatedly set forth timely affairs and suburban temple ritual, but the emperor could not use them.
translations.idiomatic: After Li Tong retired, Chen Yuan served Ouyang He and often proposed policy and ritual; the throne did not adopt his advice.

sentences.zh: 以病去，年老，卒於家。

translations.literal: Because of illness he departed; in old age he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: Illness drove him out; he died at home in old age.

sentences.zh: 子堅卿，有文章。

translations.literal: The son Jianqing had literary talent.
translations.idiomatic: His son Chen Jianqing was a fine writer.

sentences.zh: 賈逵字景伯，扶風平陵人也。

translations.literal: Jia Kui, courtesy name Jingbo, was a man of Pingling in Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Kui, style Jingbo, came from Pingling in Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 九世祖誼，文帝時為梁王太傅。

translations.literal: Nine generations back his ancestor Yi was grand tutor to the prince of Liang under Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Nine generations back, Jia Yi had been tutor to the Liang prince under Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: [一]曾祖父光，為常山太守，宣帝時以吏二千石自洛陽徙焉。

translations.literal: [One] His great-grandfather Guang was grand administrator of Changshan; in Emperor Xuan's time, as an official at two thousand shi, he moved from Luoyang thither.
translations.idiomatic: His great-grandfather Jia Guang was Changshan prefect and moved the family from Luoyang as a two-thousand-shi official.

sentences.zh: 父徽，從劉歆受左氏春秋，兼習國語、周官，又受古文尚書於塗惲，[二]學毛詩於謝曼卿，作左氏條例二十一篇。

translations.literal: His father Hui received the Zuo Spring and Autumn from Liu Xin, also studied the Discourses of the States and Rites of Zhou, and again received the old-text Documents from Tu Yun; [two] studied the Mao Odes with Xie Manqing, and composed twenty-one chapters of regulations for the Zuo commentary.
translations.idiomatic: His father Jia Hui studied Zuo under Liu Xin, Guoyu and Zhou li, old-text Documents under Tu Yun, Mao Odes under Xie Manqing, and wrote twenty-one Zuo rule chapters.

sentences.zh: 注[一]為文帝子梁王揖之傅也。

translations.literal: Note one: he was tutor to Prince Yi of Liang, son of Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Note: tutor to Emperor Wen's son at Liang.

sentences.zh: 注[二]風俗通曰：「塗姓，塗山氏之後。

translations.literal: Note two: the Fengsu tong says: 'The surname Tu descends from Mount Tu.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Fengsu tong on the Tu surname.

sentences.zh: 」惲字子真，受尚書於胡常，見前書。

translations.literal: Yun, courtesy name Zizhen, received the Documents from Hu Chang; see the former Han book.
translations.idiomatic: Tu Yun studied Documents under Hu Chang; see Hanshu.

sentences.zh: 逵悉傳父業，弱冠能誦左氏傳及五經本文，以大夏侯尚書教授，雖為古學，兼通五家谷梁之說。

translations.literal: Kui fully transmitted his father's enterprise; at the capping age he could recite the Zuo commentary and the main texts of the five classics; he taught the greater Xia Hou Documents, and though his learning was old-text, he also comprehended the theories of five houses of Guliang.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Kui inherited his father's curriculum, knew Zuo and the five classics at twenty, taught the greater Xia Hou Documents, and mastered five Guliang lineages.

sentences.zh: [一]自為兒童，常在太學，不通人閒事。

translations.literal: [One] From childhood he constantly was at the imperial academy and did not understand worldly affairs.
translations.idiomatic: From boyhood he lived at the academy and ignored the world outside.

sentences.zh: 身長八尺二寸，諸儒為之語曰：「問事不休賈長頭。

translations.literal: He was eight feet two inches tall; the various scholars made a saying of him: 'Ask questions without end—Longhead Jia.'
translations.idiomatic: At eight feet two inches he drew the rhyme: "Endless questions—Long-neck Jia."

sentences.zh: 」性愷悌，多智思，俶儻有大節。

translations.literal: His nature was gentle and kindly, he had much clever thought, and was free and easy with great integrity.
translations.idiomatic: He was genial, quick-witted, and magnanimous in principle.

sentences.zh: [二]尤明左氏傳、國語，為之解詁五十一篇，[三]永平中，上疏獻之。

translations.literal: [Two] He was especially clear on the Zuo commentary and Discourses of the States; for them he composed glosses in fifty-one chapters; [three] in the Yongping era he submitted them in a memorial.
translations.idiomatic: He especially mastered Zuo and Guoyu and wrote fifty-one chapters of glosses, presented in Yongping.

sentences.zh: 顯宗重其書，寫藏秘豫。

translations.literal: Emperor Ming valued his book, had it copied and stored in the secret treasury.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming prized the work, had it copied, and filed it in the imperial library.

sentences.zh: 注[一]五家謂尹更始、劉向、周慶、丁姓、王彥等，皆為谷梁，見前書也。

translations.literal: Note one: the five houses mean Yin Gengshi, Liu Xiang, Zhou Qing, Ding Xing, and Wang Yan, all of whom taught Guliang; see the former Han book.
translations.idiomatic: Note: the five Guliang masters listed in Hanshu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]愷，樂也。

translations.literal: Note two: kai means joy;
translations.idiomatic: Note: kai is joy;

sentences.zh: 悌，易也。

translations.literal: ti means easy.
translations.idiomatic: ti is ease—together, gentle simplicity.

sentences.zh: 言有和樂簡易之德也。

translations.literal: This speaks of having the virtue of harmony and ease.
translations.idiomatic: The phrase praises his mild temper.

sentences.zh: 俶儻，卓異也。

translations.literal: Tichang means outstanding and unusual.
translations.idiomatic: Tichang means striking and uncommon.

sentences.zh: 注[三]左氏三十篇，國語二十一篇也。

translations.literal: Note three: thirty chapters of Zuo, twenty-one of Guoyu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: thirty Zuo scrolls plus twenty-one of Guoyu.

sentences.zh: 時有神雀集宮殿官府，冠羽有五采色，帝異之，以問臨邑侯劉復，[一]復不能對，薦逵博物多識，帝乃召見逵，問之。

translations.literal: At that time divine sparrows gathered on palace halls and government offices; their crest feathers had five colors; the emperor thought it strange and asked Marquis of Linyi Liu Fu; [one] Fu could not answer, recommended Kui as broadly learned and knowing much, and the emperor therefore summoned and questioned Kui.
translations.idiomatic: Five-colored "divine sparrows" perched on halls; the emperor asked Liu Fu, who could not answer and recommended Jia Kui.

sentences.zh: 對曰：「昔武王終父之業，鸑鷟在岐，[二]宣帝威懷戎狄，神雀仍集，此胡降之征也。

translations.literal: He answered: 'Formerly King Wu completed his father's enterprise and the yuezhuo phoenix was at Qi; [two] Emperor Xuan awed and cherished the Rong and Di and divine sparrows gathered repeatedly—this is the omen of the barbarians submitting.'"
translations.idiomatic: Jia Kui replied: "King Wu finished his father's work and phoenixes came to Qi; Xuandi won the border peoples and omens repeated—your birds mean the outer tribes will yield."

sentences.zh: 」[三]帝□蘭台給筆札，使作神雀頌，拜為郎，與班固並校秘書，應對左右。

translations.literal: '[Three] The emperor ordered the Orchid Terrace to supply brush and slips, had him compose a hymn to the divine sparrows, appointed him gentleman, and together with Ban Gu collated secret books, answering to left and right.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor gave him Orchid Terrace supplies to draft an ode, made him a gentleman collator alongside Ban Gu, and kept him at call.

sentences.zh: 注[一]臨邑，東郡縣也。

translations.literal: Note one: Linyi is a county in Dong commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Linyi lay in Dong commandery.

sentences.zh: 復，齊武王伯升孫，北海王興子。

translations.literal: Fu was grandson of Prince martial of Qi Bosheng, son of Prince Xing of Beihai.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Fu was grandson of Liu Bosheng, son of the prince of Beihai.

sentences.zh: 注[二]鸑鷟，鳳之別名也。

translations.literal: Note two: yuezhuo is another name for the phoenix.
translations.idiomatic: Note: yuezhuo is a phoenix name.

sentences.zh: 周大夫內史過對周惠王曰：「周之興也，鸑鷟鳴於岐山。

translations.literal: Grandee of the interior Neishi Guo said to King Hui of Zhou: 'When Zhou rose, the yuezhuo cried on Mount Qi.'
translations.idiomatic: Neishi Guo told King Hui of Zhou that at Zhou's rise the phoenix called on Mount Qi.

sentences.zh: 」事見國語也。

translations.literal: The affair appears in the Discourses of the States.
translations.idiomatic: See Guoyu.

sentences.zh: 注[三]仍，頻也。

translations.literal: Note three: reng means repeatedly.
translations.idiomatic: Note: reng means again and again.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時神雀再見，改為年號，後匈奴降服，呼韓入朝也。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xuan's time divine sparrows appeared twice; he changed the era name; afterward the Xiongnu submitted and Huhanye entered court.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi twice saw such birds, changed his era name, and later won the Xiongnu to court.

sentences.zh: 肅宗立，降意儒術，特好古文尚書、左氏傳。

translations.literal: When Emperor Zhang was enthroned he lowered his mind to Confucian learning and especially loved the old-text Documents and Zuo commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Zhangdi favored Confucian learning, especially old-text Documents and Zuo.

sentences.zh: 建初元年，詔逵入講北宮白虎觀、南宮雲台。

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianchu an edict ordered Kui to lecture at the White Tiger Hall of the northern palace and the Cloud Terrace of the southern palace.
translations.idiomatic: Jianchu 1: he lectured at the White Tiger Hall and Cloud Terrace.

sentences.zh: 帝善逵說，使發出左氏傳大義長於二傳者。

translations.literal: The emperor approved Kui's explanations and ordered him to set forth the great meanings in which the Zuo commentary excels the two other transmissions.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked him to list where Zuo outshone Gongyang and Guliang.

sentences.zh: 逵於是具條奏之曰：

translations.literal: Kui thereupon fully listed them in a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Jia Kui then submitted:

sentences.zh: 臣謹擿出左氏三十事尤著明者，斯皆君臣之正義，父子之紀綱。

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully plucks out thirty matters of the Zuo that are especially clear—these are all correct righteousness between lord and minister and guiding norms of father and son.
translations.idiomatic: I select thirty Zuo passages that spell duty between ruler and subject and father and son.

sentences.zh: 其餘同公羊者什有七八，或文簡小異，無害大體。

translations.literal: The rest agrees with Gongyang seven or eight tenths of the time, or the wording differs slightly without harming the main body.
translations.idiomatic: Most of the rest aligns with Gongyang; minor wording shifts do not touch the core.

sentences.zh: 至如祭仲、紀季、伍子胥、叔術之屬，左氏義深於君父，父羊多任於權變，[一]其相殊絕，固以甚遠，而冤抑積久，莫肯分明。

translations.literal: As for such as Ji Zhong, Ji Ji, Wu Zixu, and Shu's ilk, the Zuo meaning is deeper toward lord and father, while Gongyang mostly trusts expedient shifts; [one] their mutual difference is indeed very far, yet wrongs have piled long with none willing to clarify them.
translations.idiomatic: Cases like Ji Zhong, Ji Ji, Wu Zixu, and Shuyi show Zuo honoring ruler and father while Gongyang leans on expediency—a gulf long left unsettled.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左傳，宋人執鄭祭仲，曰：「不立突，將死。

translations.literal: Note one: the Zuo: Song men seized Zheng's Ji Zhong, saying: 'If you do not enthrone Tu, you will die.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zuo on Ji Zhong seized by Song:

sentences.zh: 」祭仲許之，遂出昭公而立厲公。

translations.literal: Ji Zhong consented, thereupon expelled Duke Zhao and enthroned Duke Li.
translations.idiomatic: He yielded and replaced Duke Zhao (Hu) with Duke Li (Tu).

sentences.zh: 杜預注云：「祭仲之如宋，非會非聘，見誘被拘。

translations.literal: Du Yu's commentary says: 'Ji Zhong's going to Song was neither meeting nor embassy; he was lured and detained.
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu notes Ji Zhong was trapped in Song,

sentences.zh: 廢長立少，故事名罪之。」

translations.literal: Deposing the elder and enthroning the younger is therefore named a crime.'"
translations.idiomatic: and blames him for deposing the elder son."

sentences.zh: 公羊傳曰：「祭仲者何？

translations.literal: The Gongyang commentary asks: 'Who is Ji Zhong?'
translations.idiomatic: Gongyang asks who Ji Zhong is:

sentences.zh: 鄭之相也。

translations.literal: He is minister of Zheng.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng's minister.

sentences.zh: 何以不名？

translations.literal: Why is he not named?
translations.idiomatic: Why is he not named?

sentences.zh: 賢也。

translations.literal: Because he is worthy.
translations.idiomatic: Because he is worthy.

sentences.zh: 何賢乎祭仲？

translations.literal: In what is he worthy as Ji Zhong?
translations.idiomatic: What makes Ji Zhong worthy?

sentences.zh: 以為知權也。

translations.literal: In knowing expedient power.
translations.idiomatic: In knowing timely compromise.

sentences.zh: 其知權柰何？

translations.literal: What was his knowing expedient power?
translations.idiomatic: What did that expedience look like?

sentences.zh: 宋人執之，謂之曰：『為我出忽而立突。

translations.literal: Song men seized him and told him: 'For us expel Hu and enthrone Tu.'
translations.idiomatic: Song seized him and said: depose Hu for Tu.

sentences.zh: 』祭仲不從其言，則君必死，國必亡；

translations.literal: If Ji Zhong did not follow their words, then the ruler would surely die and the state surely perish;
translations.idiomatic: Refuse and the lord dies and the state falls;

sentences.zh: 從其言，則君可以生易死，國可以存易亡。

translations.literal: if he followed their words, then the ruler could trade death for life and the state trade destruction for preservation.
translations.idiomatic: obey and the ruler and state survive.

sentences.zh: 」古之有權者，祭仲之權是也。

translations.literal: 'Among the ancients who had expedient power, Ji Zhong's expedient was such.'
translations.idiomatic: Gongyang calls that true "expedient power."

sentences.zh: 左傳，紀季以酅入於齊，紀侯大去其國。

translations.literal: The Zuo: Ji Ji with Xi entered Qi; the marquis of Ji greatly abandoned his state.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo: Ji Ji surrendered Xi to Qi and Ji collapsed.

sentences.zh: 賈逵以為紀季不能兄弟同心以存國，乃背兄歸讎，書以譏之。

translations.literal: Jia Kui held that Ji Ji could not join hearts with his brother to preserve the state, but turned his back on his elder and went to an enemy; the text records this to satirize him.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Kui read Ji Ji as betraying his brother and state—Zuo records it to blame him.

sentences.zh: 公羊傳曰：「紀季者何？

translations.literal: The Gongyang commentary asks: 'Who is Ji Ji?'
translations.idiomatic: Gongyang asks who Ji Ji is:

sentences.zh: 紀侯之弟也。

translations.literal: He is the younger brother of the marquis of Ji.
translations.idiomatic: The marquis of Ji's younger brother.

sentences.zh: 何以不名？

translations.literal: Why is he not named?
translations.idiomatic: Why is he not named?

sentences.zh: 賢也。

translations.literal: Because he is worthy.
translations.idiomatic: Because he is worthy.

sentences.zh: 何賢乎？

translations.literal: In what is he worthy?
translations.idiomatic: Worthy how?

sentences.zh: 服罪也。

translations.literal: In submitting to guilt.
translations.idiomatic: In accepting guilt.

sentences.zh: 其服罪柰何？

translations.literal: What was his submitting to guilt?
translations.idiomatic: What did that submission mean?

sentences.zh: 請後五廟以存姑姊妹。

translations.literal: He begged to hand over the five later temples to preserve aunts and elder and younger sisters.'"
translations.idiomatic: He offered the secondary shrines to save his female kin."

sentences.zh: 」左傳，楚平王將殺伍奢，召伍奢子伍尚、伍員曰：「來，吾免而父。

translations.literal: The Zuo: King Ping of Chu was about to kill Wu She and summoned Wu She's sons Wu Shang and Wu Yuan, saying: 'Come—I will spare your father.'
translations.idiomatic: Zuo: King Ping summoned Wu Shang and Wu Yuan to spare their father Wu She.

sentences.zh: 」尚謂員曰：「聞免父之命，不可以莫之奔，親戚為戮，不可以莫之報。

translations.literal: Shang said to Yuan: 'Hearing the order to spare the father, one may not fail to run to it; when kin are executed, one may not fail to avenge it.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Shang told Wu Yuan they must answer the summons yet avenge kin,

sentences.zh: 父不可□，名不可廢。」

translations.literal: The father cannot be cast aside, the name cannot be discarded.'"
translations.idiomatic: for a father cannot be abandoned nor honor thrown away."

sentences.zh: 子胥奔吳，遂以吳師入郢，卒復父讎。

translations.literal: Zixu fled to Wu, thereupon led Wu troops into Ying, and in the end avenged his father's enemy.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Yuan fled to Wu, brought its army to Ying, and avenged his father.

sentences.zh: 公羊傳曰：「父受誅，子復讎，推刃之道也。

translations.literal: The Gongyang commentary says: 'When the father suffers execution, the son avenges—this is the way of mutual blades.'
translations.idiomatic: Gongyang allows sons to avenge executed fathers.

sentences.zh: 」公羊不許子胥復讎，是不深父也。

translations.literal: Gongyang does not permit Zixu to avenge—this is not deep toward the father.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Gongyang would block Zixu's revenge—too shallow on filial duty.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「冬，邾黑肱以濫來奔。

translations.literal: The Zuo says: 'In winter Heigong of Zhu with Lan came to flight.'
translations.idiomatic: Zuo: Zhu's Heigong fled with the Lan district.

sentences.zh: 賤而書名，重地故也。

translations.literal: Because he was base yet is named, it is because the territory weighs heavily.
translations.idiomatic: He is named despite low rank because land matters.

sentences.zh: 君子曰：『名之不可不慎。

translations.literal: The gentleman said: 'Names must not be treated carelessly.
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman warns: names must be chosen with care,

sentences.zh: 』以地叛，雖賤必書。

translations.literal: To rebel with territory, though base, must be written.
translations.idiomatic: for revolt tied to territory is always recorded,

sentences.zh: 地以名其人，終為不義，不可滅已。

translations.literal: The territory thereby names the man; in the end it is unrighteous and cannot be extinguished.
translations.idiomatic: and the place brands the man forever as unjust.

sentences.zh: 是以君子動則思禮，行則思義。

translations.literal: Therefore the gentleman in moving thinks of ritual, in acting thinks of righteousness.'"
translations.idiomatic: So the gentleman weighs ritual and righteousness in every act."

sentences.zh: 」公羊傳曰：「冬，黑弓以濫來奔，文何以無邾婁？

translations.literal: The Gongyang commentary says: 'In winter Black Bow with Lan came to flight—why does the text not have Zhu Lou?
translations.idiomatic: Gongyang on Black Bow fleeing to Lu with Lan:

sentences.zh: 通濫也。

translations.literal: It communicates Lan.
translations.idiomatic: It treats Lan as an open enclave.

sentences.zh: 曷*

translations.literal: He—
translations.idiomatic: The damaged text reads "why"—

translations.literal: (gloss)
translations.idiomatic: an editor's gloss,

translations.literal: **[why]* communicate Lan?
translations.idiomatic: asking why Lan is treated as freely joined,

sentences.zh: 賢者子孫宜有地。

translations.literal: The worthy man's descendants ought to have land.
translations.idiomatic: because a sage's heirs deserve a fief.

sentences.zh: 賢者孰謂？

translations.literal: Which worthy man?
translations.idiomatic: Which sage?

sentences.zh: 謂叔術也。

translations.literal: It means Shuyi.
translations.idiomatic: Shuyi of Zhu.

sentences.zh: 何賢乎叔術？

translations.literal: In what is Shuyi worthy?
translations.idiomatic: Why praise Shuyi?

sentences.zh: 讓國也。」

translations.literal: In yielding the state.'"
translations.idiomatic: For yielding his state."

sentences.zh: 臣以永平中上言左氏與圖讖合者，先帝不遺芻蕘，省納臣言，寫其傳詁，藏之秘書。

translations.literal: Your servant in the Yongping era submitted that the Zuo matched the charts and weft-texts; the late emperor did not despise straw and firewood opinion, examined and accepted your servant's words, copied its transmitted glosses, and stored them in the secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping I showed Zuo's fit with prophecy; the late emperor accepted my glosses into the imperial library.

sentences.zh: 建平中，[一]侍中劉歆欲立左氏，不先暴論大義，而輕移太常，恃其義長，詆挫諸儒，諸儒內懷不服，相與排之。

translations.literal: In the Jianping era, [one] attendant-in-chief Liu Xin wished to establish the Zuo; without first publishing the great meanings he lightly shifted the grand master of ceremonies, relied on his doctrine's superiority, slandered and crushed the various scholars, who inwardly nursed discontent and jointly rejected him.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianping Liu Xin pushed a Zuo chair without debate, bullied other scholars, and earned their united opposition.

sentences.zh: [二]孝哀皇帝重逆觿心，故出歆為河內太守。

translations.literal: [Two] Emperor Xiaoai, weighing the multitude's hearts, therefore sent Xin out as grand administrator of Henei.
translations.idiomatic: Aidi bowed to opinion and banished Liu Xin to Henei.

sentences.zh: 從是攻擊左氏，遂為重讎。

translations.literal: From this attacks on the Zuo became a deep feud.
translations.idiomatic: Since then Zuo and orthodox schools have been at war.

sentences.zh: 至光武皇帝，奮獨見之明，興立左氏、谷梁，會二家先師不曉圖讖，故令中道而廢。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Guangwu, who roused singular insight and raised the Zuo and Guliang, because the founding masters of the two houses did not understand charts and weft-texts, he ordered them halted midway.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu favored Zuo and Guliang but dropped both when their teachers flubbed prophecy.

sentences.zh: 凡所以存先王之道者，要在安上理民也。

translations.literal: In general what preserves the way of former kings lies chiefly in securing superiors and ordering the people.
translations.idiomatic: Preserving the ancient way means stabilizing ruler and people.

sentences.zh: 今左氏崇君父，卑臣子，強幹弱枝，勸善戒惡，至明至切，至直至順。

translations.literal: Now the Zuo exalts lord and father, demeans minister and son, strengthens the trunk and weakens branches, urges good and warns against evil—it is supremely clear, supremely cutting, supremely straight, supremely compliant.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo exalts ruler and father, humbles minister and son, strengthens root over branch, and teaches good against evil—lucid, urgent, upright, and orderly.

sentences.zh: [三]且三代異物，損益隨時，故先帝博觀異家，各有所採。

translations.literal: [Three] Moreover the three ages had different institutions, trimming and adding followed the times; therefore former emperors broadly viewed different houses and each took something.
translations.idiomatic: Three dynasties changed ritual; wise emperors sampled every school.

sentences.zh: 易有施、孟，復立梁丘，[四]尚書歐陽，復有大小夏侯，[五]今三傳之異亦猶是也。

translations.literal: In the Changes there were Shi and Meng, and Liangqiu was again raised; [four] in the Documents there was Ouyang, and again greater and lesser Xia Hou were raised; [five] the divergence of the three Spring and Autumn transmissions is likewise.
translations.idiomatic: Changes had Shi and Meng, then Liangqiu; Documents had Ouyang plus two Xia Hou—the three Annals differ no more than that.

sentences.zh: 又五經家皆無以證圖讖明劉氏為堯後者，而左氏獨有明文。

translations.literal: Moreover none of the five-classics houses has anything in the charts and weft-texts to prove that the Liu house descends from Yao, while the Zuo alone has explicit text.
translations.idiomatic: No other classic proves Han's descent from Yao in the weft-books—only Zuo states it plainly.

sentences.zh: [六]五經家皆言顓頊代黃帝，而堯不得為火德。

translations.literal: [Six] The five-classics houses all say Zhuanxu succeeded the Yellow Thearch, so Yao cannot be fire virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Orthodox schools make Zhuanxu follow the Yellow Thearch, which would deny Yao the fire phase.

sentences.zh: [七]左氏以為少昊代黃帝，即圖讖所謂帝宣也。

translations.literal: [Seven] The Zuo holds that Shaohao succeeded the Yellow Thearch—that is the Di Xuan whom the charts and weft-texts mean.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo makes Shaohao follow Huangdi—the weft-texts' "Emperor Xuan."

sentences.zh: [八]如令堯不得為火，則漢不得為赤。

translations.literal: [Eight] If Yao may not be fire, then Han may not be red.
translations.idiomatic: Deny Yao fire and you deny Han's red Mandate.

sentences.zh: 其所發明，補益實多。

translations.literal: What it brings to light truly adds much solid benefit.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo's glosses fill real gaps in the canon.

sentences.zh: 注[一]建平，哀帝年也。

translations.literal: Note one: Jianping is an era of Emperor Ai.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Jianping was Aidi's reign.

sentences.zh: 注[二]排，擯□也。

translations.literal: Note two: pai means to reject and push away.
translations.idiomatic: Note: pai means to ostracize.

sentences.zh: 劉歆欲建立左氏，哀帝令歆與諸儒講論其義，諸博士不肯置對，歆乃移書太常以責之，故被排擯。

translations.literal: Liu Xin wished to establish the Zuo; Emperor Ai ordered Xin to debate its meaning with the various scholars; the various erudites would not answer; Xin then sent a letter to the grand master of ceremonies to reproach them, therefore he was rejected and pushed aside.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xin asked for a Zuo chair; Aidi told him to debate the erudites, who refused to reply, so Xin blasted the grand master of ceremonies and was shunned.

sentences.zh: 事見前書。

translations.literal: The affair appears in the former Han book.
translations.idiomatic: See Hanshu.

sentences.zh: 注[三]左傳曰：「翼戴天子，加之以恭。

translations.literal: Note three: the Zuo says: 'Wing and uphold the Son of Heaven, adding reverence to him.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zuo on supporting the Son of Heaven,

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「君命，天也，天可讎乎？

translations.literal: It also says: 'The lord's command is heaven—may heaven be opposed?'
translations.idiomatic: and on the lord's command as heaven,

sentences.zh: 委質策名，貳乃辟也。

translations.literal: To pledge body and inscribe name, then to turn aside is crime.
translations.idiomatic: pledging service yet rebelling,

sentences.zh: 父教子貳，何以事君？

translations.literal: If the father teaches the son to turn aside, how shall he serve the lord?
translations.idiomatic: fathers teaching sons to rebel,

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「□父之命，惡用子矣，以有無父之國則可。

translations.literal: It also says: 'To cast aside a father's command—of what use is the son? Only in a land without fathers would it do.'
translations.idiomatic: and on rejecting a father's order—this is how Zuo exalts ruler and father.

sentences.zh: 」是崇君父，卑臣子也。

translations.literal: This is exalting lord and father and demeaning minister and son.
translations.idiomatic: Those lines exalt sovereign and parent over subject and child.

sentences.zh: 左氏王人雖微，序在諸侯之上。

translations.literal: In the Zuo, royal men though petty are ordered above the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo ranks even minor Zhou envoys above feudal rulers.

sentences.zh: 又曰：

translations.literal: It also says:
translations.idiomatic: It continues:

sentences.zh: 「五大不在邊，五細不在庭，末大必折，尾大不掉。

translations.literal: 'The five great may not be on the border, the five petty may not be in the court; when the tip is large it must snap, when the tail is large it cannot wag.'
translations.idiomatic: "Great power must not sit on the frontier, petty power not in the inner court—oversized branches break, heavy tails cannot wag."

sentences.zh: 」是強幹弱枝也。

translations.literal: This is strengthening the trunk and weakening branches.
translations.idiomatic: That is the doctrine of strong center, weak periphery.

sentences.zh: 又曰：「盡而不污，懲惡而勸善，非聖人誰能修之？

translations.literal: It also says: 'To exhaust without filth, punish evil and urge good—who but a sage can compile that?'
translations.idiomatic: It praises the Annals for punishing vice and praising virtue—work only a sage could edit,

sentences.zh: 」史記曰，孔子曰：「我欲載之空言，不如見之行事深切著明也。」

translations.literal: ' The Records say Confucius said: 'I would rather set it forth in deeds than in empty words, for deeds touch deeply and show clearly.'"
translations.idiomatic: and cites Confucius preferring deeds to empty speech."

sentences.zh: 注[四]施讎、孟喜、梁丘賀也。

translations.literal: Note four: Shi Chou, Meng Xi, and Liangqiu He.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Shi, Meng, and Liangqiu Changes masters.

sentences.zh: 注[五]歐陽和伯、大夏侯勝、小夏侯建也。

translations.literal: Note five: Ouyang Hebo, greater Xia Hou Sheng, lesser Xia Hou Jian.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Ouyang and two Xia Hou Documents lines.

sentences.zh: 並見前書。

translations.literal: All appear in the former Han book.
translations.idiomatic: All are in Hanshu.

sentences.zh: 注[六]春秋晉大夫蔡墨曰：「陶唐氏既衰，其後有劉累，學擾龍，事孔甲，范氏其後也。

translations.literal: Note six: Cai Mo, grandee of Jin, said in the Spring and Autumn: 'When the Tao Tang line declined, their descendant was Liu Lei, who learned to train dragons and served Kong Jia—the Fan line is their posterity.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zuo quote on Liu Lei, dragon-tamer, ancestor of Fan,

sentences.zh: 」范會自秦還晉，其處者為劉氏。

translations.literal: Fan Hui returned from Qin to Jin; those who stayed behind became the Liu house.
translations.idiomatic: and Fan Hui's branch that became the Liu clan—proving Han follows Yao.

sentences.zh: 明漢承堯後也。

translations.literal: This clarifies that Han inherits from Yao.
translations.idiomatic: That is the Han-from-Yao proof text.

sentences.zh: 注[七]史記曰「黃帝崩，其孫昌意之子立，是為帝顓頊」。

translations.literal: Note seven: the Records says: 'When the Yellow Thearch died, his grandson, the son of Changyi, succeeded—this is Emperor Zhuanxu.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Shiji succession Huangdi to Zhuanxu,

sentences.zh: 當時五經家同為此說。

translations.literal: At that time the five-classics houses agreed on this reading.
translations.idiomatic: which the old schools all accepted.

sentences.zh: 若以顓頊代黃帝以土德王，即顓頊當為金德，高辛為水德，堯為木德。

translations.literal: If Zhuanxu replaced the Yellow Thearch as king by earth virtue, then Zhuanxu ought to be metal virtue, Gao Xin water, Yao wood.
translations.idiomatic: That scheme makes Zhuanxu earth, then metal, water, wood for later rulers,

sentences.zh: 漢承堯後，自然不得為火德也。

translations.literal: Han inheriting from Yao naturally could not be fire virtue.
translations.idiomatic: which would block Han from claiming fire from Yao.

sentences.zh: 注[八]左氏傳曰：「黃帝氏以雲紀，少昊氏以鳥紀。

translations.literal: Note eight: the Zuo commentary says: 'The Yellow Thearch clan used clouds as titles; the Shaohao clan used birds as titles.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zuo contrasts Huangdi's cloud offices with Shaohao's bird offices,

sentences.zh: 」是以少昊代黃帝也。

translations.literal: Therefore Shaohao replaced the Yellow Thearch.
translations.idiomatic: so Shaohao succeeds Huangdi in Zuo's genealogy.

sentences.zh: 河圖曰：「大星如虹，下流華渚，女節意感，生白帝朱宣。

translations.literal: The River Chart says: 'A great star like a rainbow flowed down to Hua islet; Lady Jie was moved and bore the white thearch Zhu Xuan.'
translations.idiomatic: The Hetu names Zhu Xuan, the white thearch,

sentences.zh: 」宋均注曰：「朱宣，少昊氏也。」

translations.literal: Song Jun's commentary says: 'Zhu Xuan is the Shaohao clan.'"
translations.idiomatic: and Song Jun identifies him with Shaohao."

sentences.zh: 陛下通天然之明，建大聖之本，改元正歷，垂萬世則，[一]是以麟鳳百數，嘉瑞雜沓。

translations.literal: Your Majesty penetrates heaven-endowed clarity, establishes the root of great sageliness, has changed the era title and corrected the calendar, and hung a model for ten thousand generations; [one] therefore unicorns and phoenixes come by the hundreds and auspicious signs crowd in profusion.
translations.idiomatic: Your clarity and calendar reform draw prodigies in flocks—unicorns, phoenixes, and more.

sentences.zh: [二]猶朝夕恪勤，游情六蓺，研機綜微，靡不審核。

translations.literal: [Two] Still morning and evening you are diligent and careful, roam your mind in the six arts, examine subtleties and weave the fine—nothing is not verified.
translations.idiomatic: Yet you still toil day and night in the six arts, weighing every subtle point.

sentences.zh: [三]若復留意廢學，以廣聖見，庶幾無所遺失矣。

translations.literal: [Three] If you again attend to the neglected learning and broaden sage insight, perhaps nothing will be left out.
translations.idiomatic: Turn again to the long-neglected Zuo and your vision of the canon will miss nothing.

sentences.zh: [四] 注[一]改元謂改建初九年為元和元年，正歷謂元和二年始用四分歷也。

translations.literal: [Four] Note one: changing the era title means altering Jianchu 9 to Yuanhe 1; correcting the calendar means that in Yuanhe 2 the quarter-day calendar first came into use.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yuanhe era change and adoption of the quarter-day calendar.

sentences.zh: 注[二]雜沓言多也。

translations.literal: Note two: za ta means many.
translations.idiomatic: Note: za ta means numerous.

sentences.zh: 章帝時，鳳皇見百三十九，騏麟五十二，白虎二十九，黃龍三十四，神雀、白燕等史官不可勝紀。

translations.literal: In Emperor Zhang's time phoenixes appeared 139 times, qilin fifty-two, white tigers twenty-nine, yellow dragons thirty-four, divine sparrows and white swallows—the historiographers could not record them all.
translations.idiomatic: Zhangdi's reign logged 139 phoenix sightings, 52 qilin, 29 white tigers, 34 yellow dragons, and countless smaller omens.

sentences.zh: 見東觀記。

translations.literal: See the Eastern View record.
translations.idiomatic: See Dongguan ji.

sentences.zh: 注[三]核，實也。

translations.literal: Note three: he means solid.
translations.idiomatic: Note: he means verified or solid.

sentences.zh: 注[四]廢學謂左氏傳也。

translations.literal: Note four: neglected learning means the Zuo commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "neglected learning" is the Zuo tradition.

sentences.zh: 書奏，帝嘉之，賜布五百匹，衣一襲，令逵自選公羊嚴、顏諸生高才者二十人，教以左氏，[一]與簡紙經傳各一通。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted the emperor praised it, gifted five hundred bolts of cloth and one suit of clothes, ordered Kui himself to select twenty talented students of Gongyang from the Yan and Yan lineages, and teach them the Zuo; [one] he also gave each one set of bamboo slips and paper with classic and commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Zhangdi rewarded Jia Kui and told him to teach twenty picked Gongyang students the Zuo text, with paper and slips.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]公羊高作春秋傳，號曰公羊春秋。

translations.literal: [Two] Note one: Gao Yang made the Spring and Autumn commentary called Gongyang Spring and Autumn.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gongyang Gao's tradition,

sentences.zh: 嚴彭祖、顏安樂俱受公羊春秋，故公羊有嚴、顏之學。

translations.literal: Yan Pengzu and Yan Anle both received Gongyang Spring and Autumn, therefore Gongyang has the Yan and Yan learnings.
translations.idiomatic: carried by Yan Pengzu and Yan Anle—hence two Yan schools.

sentences.zh: 見前書也。

translations.literal: See the former Han book.
translations.idiomatic: See Hanshu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]竹簡及紙也。

translations.literal: Note two: bamboo slips and paper.
translations.idiomatic: Note: gifts included bamboo and paper.

sentences.zh: 逵母常有疾，帝欲加賜，以校書例多，特以錢二十萬，使穎陽侯馬防與之。

translations.literal: Kui's mother was often ill; the emperor wished to add gifts, but because the precedent for collators was much, he specially gave two hundred thousand cash and ordered marquis Ma Fang of Yingyang to deliver it.
translations.idiomatic: His mother fell ill; bypassing usual collator rules the emperor sent Ma Fang with two hundred thousand cash.

sentences.zh: 謂防曰：「賈逵母病，此子無人事於外，[一]屢空則從孤竹之子於首陽山矣。

translations.literal: He told Fang: 'Jia Kui's mother is ill; this son has no human affairs outside; [one] if repeatedly empty he would follow the sons of Guzhu to Mount Shouyang.'
translations.idiomatic: He told Ma Fang: "Jia Kui keeps no outside ties—if he goes broke he will starve like Bo Yi and Shu Qi."

sentences.zh: 」[二] 注[一]無人事謂不廣交通也。

translations.literal: '[Two] Note one: no human affairs means he does not widely traffic in connections.
translations.idiomatic: Note: he did not network for gain,

sentences.zh: 注[二]屢，數也。

translations.literal: Note two: lu means repeatedly.
translations.idiomatic: lu means often,

sentences.zh: 空，乏也。

translations.literal: kong means destitute.
translations.idiomatic: kong means destitute.

sentences.zh: 史記曰，伯夷、叔齊，孤竹君之子也，隱於首陽山，卒餓死也。

translations.literal: The Records say that Bo Yi and Shu Qi were sons of the lord of Guzhu, hid on Mount Shouyang, and in the end starved to death.
translations.idiomatic: The allusion is to Bo Yi and Shu Qi dying of hunger on Shouyang.

sentences.zh: 逵數為帝言古文尚書與經傳爾雅詁訓相應，詔令撰歐陽、大小夏侯尚書古文同異。

translations.literal: Kui repeatedly spoke to the emperor that the old-text Documents matched the classics, commentaries, and Erya glosses; an edict ordered him to compile the sameness and difference of Ouyang and greater and lesser Xia Hou Documents with the old text.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Kui showed old-text Documents matched Erya; the court had him collate Ouyang and Xia Hou against the old-text.

sentences.zh: 逵集為三卷，帝善之。

translations.literal: Kui collected them in three fascicles; the emperor approved.
translations.idiomatic: He produced three fascicles to the throne's praise.

sentences.zh: 復令撰齊、魯、韓詩與毛氏異同。

translations.literal: He was again ordered to compile the sameness and difference of Qi, Lu, and Han Odes with the Mao version.
translations.idiomatic: Next he compared Qi, Lu, and Han Odes to Mao.

sentences.zh: 並作周官解故。

translations.literal: He also composed explications of the Rites of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: He also glossed the Rites of Zhou.

sentences.zh: [一] 遷逵為□士令。

translations.literal: [One] Kui was promoted to director of the palace gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted director of palace gentlemen,

sentences.zh: [二]八年，乃詔諸儒各選高才生，受左氏、谷梁春秋、古文尚書、毛詩，由是四經遂行於世。

translations.literal: [Two] In the eighth year an edict then ordered the various scholars each to select talented students to receive Zuo and Guliang Spring and Autumn, old-text Documents, and Mao Odes—thereby the four classics went into practice in the world.
translations.idiomatic: and in year 8 ordered high scholars taught Zuo, Guliang, old Documents, and Mao—so four texts spread.

sentences.zh: 皆拜逵所選弟子及門生為千乘王國郎，[三]朝夕受業黃門署，學者皆欣欣羨慕焉。

translations.literal: All the disciples and gate students Kui had chosen were appointed gentlemen to the kingdom of Qiansheng; [three] morning and evening they received instruction at the yellow gate office, and students all admired them joyfully.
translations.idiomatic: His picks became gentlemen to Prince Qiansheng and studied at the yellow gate—every scholar envied them.

sentences.zh: 注[一]轅固，齊人也，為齊詩；

translations.literal: Note one: Yuan Gu of Qi taught Qi odes;
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yuan Gu's Qi odes,

sentences.zh: 申公，魯人也，為魯詩；

translations.literal: Shen Gong of Lu taught Lu odes;
translations.idiomatic: Shen Gong's Lu odes,

sentences.zh: 韓嬰為韓詩；

translations.literal: Han Ying taught Han odes;
translations.idiomatic: Han Ying's Han odes,

sentences.zh: 毛萇為毛詩。

translations.literal: Mao Chang taught Mao odes.
translations.idiomatic: Mao Chang's Mao odes.

sentences.zh: 故謂事之指意也。

translations.literal: Therefore it means the pointed meaning of the affair.
translations.idiomatic: Hence "pointed meaning" of the texts.

sentences.zh: 注[二]北宮□士令一人，掌南、北宮，秩比六百石，見續漢志也。

translations.literal: Note two: the Northern Palace director of gentlemen, one man, manages south and north palaces, rank comparable to six hundred shi; see the continued Han treatise.
translations.idiomatic: Note: the director of palace gentlemen at six hundred shi rank.

sentences.zh: 注[三]千乘王伉，章帝子也。

translations.literal: Note three: Prince Kang of Qiansheng was a son of Emperor Zhang.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Qiansheng prince was Zhangdi's son.

sentences.zh: 和帝即位，永元三年，以逵為左中郎將。

translations.literal: When Emperor He took the throne, in the third year of Yongyuan he appointed Kui general of the household for the left.
translations.idiomatic: Hedi made him general of the household for the left in Yongyuan 3.

sentences.zh: 八年，復為侍中，領騎都尉。

translations.literal: In the eighth year he again became attendant-in-chief and concurrently colonel of cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Yongyuan 8 brought attendant-in-chief and cavalry colonel.

sentences.zh: 內備帷幄，兼領秘書近署，甚見信用。

translations.literal: Within he prepared the curtain strategy and concurrently led the nearby secretariat—he was greatly trusted.
translations.idiomatic: He advised in the privy council and ran the palace library—deeply trusted.

sentences.zh: 逵薦東萊司馬均、陳國汝郁，帝即征之，並蒙優禮。

translations.literal: Kui recommended Sima Jun of Donglai and Yu Yu of Chen; the emperor at once summoned them and both received generous courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: He recommended Sima Jun and Yu Yu; both were summoned and honored.

sentences.zh: 均字少賓，安貧好學，隱居教授，不應辟命。

translations.literal: Jun, courtesy name Shaobin, loved poverty and study, lived in reclusion teaching, and would not answer summons.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Jun, style Shaobin, was a poor recluse who refused office.

sentences.zh: 信誠行乎州裡，鄉人有所計爭，輒令祝少賓，[一]不直者終無敢言。

translations.literal: Good faith moved his commandery; when fellow villagers had reckonings and disputes they would order them to swear by Shaobin—those not in the right in the end dared not speak.
translations.idiomatic: His village trusted him: the unjust dared not invoke his name in oaths.

sentences.zh: 位至侍中，以老病乞身，帝賜以大夫祿，歸鄉里。

translations.literal: He rose to attendant-in-chief; because of old age and illness he begged his person; the emperor granted him grandee's salary and returned him to his village.
translations.idiomatic: He retired ill as attendant-in-chief with a grandee's stipend.

sentences.zh: 郁字叔異，性仁孝，[二]及親歿，遂隱處山澤。

translations.literal: Yu, courtesy name Shuyi, was humane and filial; [two] when his parents died he then hid in mountains and marshes.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Yu, style Shuyi, mourned his parents in mountain seclusion,

sentences.zh: 後累遷為魯相，以德教化，百姓稱之，流人歸者八九千戶。

translations.literal: Later he was repeatedly promoted and became grand administrator of Lu, transforming the people by virtue; the common people praised him, and wandering households who returned numbered eight or nine thousand.
translations.idiomatic: then governed Lu so well that eight or nine thousand refugee households came home.

sentences.zh: 注[一]祝，詛也。

translations.literal: Note one: zhu means curse.
translations.idiomatic: Note: zhu means to swear an oath or curse.

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰：「爭曲直者，輒言『敢祝少賓乎』？

translations.literal: The Eastern View record says: 'Those who disputed right and wrong would say "Do you dare swear by Shaobin?"
translations.idiomatic: Dongguan ji: litigants dared not swear falsely by Sima Jun,

sentences.zh: 心不直者，終不敢祝也。」

translations.literal: Those whose hearts were not straight in the end did not dare to swear.'"
translations.idiomatic: for the crooked feared his name."

sentences.zh: 注[二]東觀記曰：「郁年五歲，母病不能食，郁常抱持啼泣，亦不食。

translations.literal: Note two: the Eastern View record says: 'When Yu was five, his mother was ill and could not eat; Yu constantly embraced her weeping and would not eat either.
translations.idiomatic: Note: at five Yu Yu fasted with his sick mother,

sentences.zh: 母憐之，強為飯。

translations.literal: His mother pitied him and forced him to eat.
translations.idiomatic: until she forced food on him,

sentences.zh: 宗親共異之，因字曰『異』也。」

translations.literal: The whole clan marveled and therefore gave him the courtesy name "Yi."'"
translations.idiomatic: and the clan gave him the style "Yi" for his odd devotion."

sentences.zh: 逵所著經傳義詁及論難百餘萬言，又作詩、頌、誄、書、連珠、酒令凡九篇，學者宗之，後世稱為通儒。

translations.literal: What Kui wrote in glosses on classics and commentaries and disputations ran to more than a million graphs; he also composed in all nine pieces of odes, hymns, dirges, letters, linked pearls, and drinking rules—students took him as authority and later ages called him a thorough scholar.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Kui's corpus passed a million words, plus nine literary pieces—he became the model "thorough scholar."

sentences.zh: [一]然不修小節，當世以此頗譏焉，故不至大官。

translations.literal: [One] Yet he did not cultivate small points; the age for this somewhat ridiculed him, therefore he did not reach high office.
translations.idiomatic: He scorned petty etiquette, which cost him top posts.

sentences.zh: 永元十三年卒，時年七十二。

translations.literal: He died in the thirteenth year of Yongyuan at age seventy-two.
translations.idiomatic: He died in Yongyuan 13 at seventy-two.

sentences.zh: 朝廷愍惜，除兩子為太子舍人。

translations.literal: The court pitied him and appointed two sons gentlemen attendants to the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: The court mourned him and made two sons attendants to the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 注[一]應劭風俗通義曰：「授先王之制，立當時之事，綱紀國體，原本要化，此通儒也。」

translations.literal: Note one: Ying Shao's Fengsu tongyi says: 'To transmit the former kings' institutions, establish the affairs of the present, order the state's body, trace origins to essential transformation—this is the thorough scholar.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: Ying Shao's definition of a tongru.

sentences.zh: 論曰：鄭、賈之學，行乎數百年中，遂為諸儒宗，亦徒有以焉爾。

translations.literal: The judgment says: the learning of Zheng and Jia held sway for several hundred years among scholars and became patriarch to the Ru—yet they had only this to recommend them.
translations.idiomatic: The historian writes: Zheng and Jia dominated learning for centuries—yet little else won the throne's love.

sentences.zh: [一]桓譚以不善讖流亡，鄭興以遜辭僅免，賈逵能附會文致，最差貴顯。

translations.literal: [One] Huan Tan because he was not good at prognostications wandered in exile; Zheng Xing by yielding words barely escaped; Jia Kui could attach and elaborate texts and was the most honored and prominent.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Tan fled for spurning chenwei; Zheng Xing barely survived by tact; Jia Kui thrived by fitting Zuo to prophecy,

sentences.zh: [二]世主以此論學，悲矣哉！

translations.literal: [Two] for rulers of the age to judge learning by this—how sad!
translations.idiomatic: yet emperors judged scholars by weft-text skill—sad business,

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]言賈、鄭雖為儒宗，而不為帝所重，故曰「亦徒有以焉爾」。

translations.literal: [Three] Note one: this says that though Jia and Zheng were patriarchs to the Ru they were not heavily valued by the emperor—hence 'they had only this to recommend them.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: the verdict is that emperors valued omen-matching over true scholarship.

sentences.zh: 注[二]賈逵附會文致，謂引左氏明漢為堯後也。

translations.literal: Note two: Jia Kui's attaching and elaborating texts means citing the Zuo to show Han as Yao's descendant.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "attach texts" means the Han-from-Yao argument.

sentences.zh: 注[三]言時主不重經而重讖也。

translations.literal: Note three: this says rulers of the time did not value the classics but valued prognostications.
translations.idiomatic: Note: the age preferred prophecy to canon.

sentences.zh: 張霸字伯饒，蜀郡成都人也。

translations.literal: Zhang Ba, courtesy name Borao, was a man of Chengdu in Shu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ba, style Borao, came from Chengdu in Shu.

sentences.zh: 年數歲而知孝讓，雖出入飲食，自然合禮，鄉人號為「張曾子。

translations.literal: At a few years of age he knew filial yielding; though going out and in for drink and food, naturally he accorded with ritual, and fellow villagers styled him 'Zengzi Zhang.'
translations.idiomatic: As a child he was so deferential that neighbors called him "the young Zengzi."

sentences.zh: 」七歲通春秋，復欲進余經，父母曰「汝小未能也」，霸曰「我饒為之」，故字曰「饒」焉。

translations.literal: At seven he mastered the Spring and Autumn and wished to advance to other classics; his parents said 'You are small and cannot yet,' but Ba said 'I have plenty to do with them,' therefore his courtesy name was taken as Rao.
translations.idiomatic: At seven he knew the Annals and pressed for more classics; his parents said he was too young, but he said he had plenty of capacity—hence the style Rao.

sentences.zh: 注[一]饒猶益也。

translations.literal: Note one: rao means to have surplus.
translations.idiomatic: Note: rao means abundance or surplus.

sentences.zh: 後就長水校尉樊*

translations.literal: Later he studied under colonel of the Chang waters Fan (reading shu) and received Yan's Gongyang Spring and Autumn from him, thereupon broadly perused the five classics.
translations.idiomatic: He studied under Fan Shu, colonel of the Chang water regiment, took Yan Pengzu's Gongyang Annals, and then mastered the five classics.

translations.literal: (shu)
translations.idiomatic: Alternate reading shu for the teacher's name Fan.

sentences.zh: **[鯈]*受嚴氏公羊春秋，遂博覽五經。

translations.literal: **[鯈]* received Yan's Gongyang Spring and Autumn from him, thereupon broadly perused the five classics.
translations.idiomatic: He completed his study under Fan Shu with Yan's Gongyang and then read the five classics widely.

sentences.zh: 諸生孫林、劉固、段著等慕之，各市宅其傍，以就學焉。

translations.literal: Students such as Sun Lin, Liu Gu, and Duan Zhu admired him; each bought a house beside him to attend study.
translations.idiomatic: Disciples like Sun Lin, Liu Gu, and Duan Zhu bought houses next to his to study.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉光祿主事，稍遷，[一]永元中為會稽太守，表用郡人處士顧奉、公孫松等。

translations.literal: He was recommended as filial and incorrupt and served as chief clerk to the minister of the imperial household, was gradually promoted, [one] and in the Yongyuan era became grand administrator of Kuaiji, where he memorialized to employ recluses of the commandery such as Gu Feng and Gongsun Song.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended filial and honest, he rose to Kuaiji prefect in Yongyuan and appointed local worthies Gu Feng and Gongsun Song.

sentences.zh: 奉後為穎川太守，松為司隸校尉，並有名稱。

translations.literal: Feng later became grand administrator of Yingchuan, Song metropolitan commandant—both had celebrated reputations.
translations.idiomatic: Gu Feng became Yingchuan prefect and Gongsun Song metropolitan commandant, both famous.

sentences.zh: 其餘有業行者，皆見擢用。

translations.literal: The rest who had conduct of affairs were all promoted and employed.
translations.idiomatic: Others with merit were promoted too.

sentences.zh: 郡中爭厲志節，習經者以千數，道路但聞誦聲。

translations.literal: In the commandery they vied to sharpen resolve and integrity; those who studied the classics numbered in the thousands, and on the roads one heard only recitation voices.
